elements.sent-2411-001: Averaging iterations of Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) have achieved empirical success in training deep learning models, such as Stochastic Weight Averaging (SWA), Exponential Moving Average (EMA), and LAtest Weight Averaging (LAWA).
elements.sent-2411-002: Especially, with a finite weight averaging method, LAWA can attain faster convergence and better generalization.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, its theoretical explanation is still less explored since there are fundamental differences between finite and infinite settings.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this work, we first generalize SGD and LAWA as Finite Weight Averaging (FWA) and explain their advantages compared to SGD from the perspective of optimization and generalization.
elements.sent-2411-005: A key challenge is the inapplicability of traditional methods in the sense of expectation or optimal values for infinite-dimensional settings in analyzing FWA's convergence.
elements.sent-2411-006: Second, the cumulative gradients introduced by FWA introduce additional confusion to the generalization analysis, especially making it more difficult to discuss them under different assumptions.

elements.sent-2411-012: Weight averaging methods have shown great success in diverse applications ranging from convex to non-convex optimization.
elements.sent-2411-013: From the perspective of geometric intuition, a converging optimizer fluctuates around a minimum value, so weight-averaging methods tend to approach the minimum.
elements.sent-2411-014: In the 90s, \citet{polyak1992acceleration have proven that averaging the model weights can accelerate the convergence speed in the convex loss function regime.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Now, in deep learning, the prevalence of large models often results in high-dimensional loss functions and non-convex, which lead to the creation of many variants of averaging methods, such as Exponential Moving Average (EMA) \citep{szegedy2016rethinking , Stochastic Weight Averaging (SWA) \citep{izmailov2018averaging , LAtest Weight Averaging (LAWA) \citep{kaddour2022stop,sanyal2023early .}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: These methods are widely used for training foundation models and have been empirically shown to perform well.
elements.sent-2411-017: The most commonly held geometric intuition is that all the averaging techniques aim to achieve a flat landscape, which yields a better generalization.

elements.sent-2411-051: Our first theoretical investigation focuses on algorithms such as LAWA, and we provide a theoretical explanation for its more general form, FWA.
elements.sent-2411-052: We elucidate why it exhibits better generalization and faster convergence compared to SGD.

elements.sent-2411-255: Below, we conduct several experiments to verify our derived optimization and generalization bound, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-256: All experiments are conducted using the NVIDIA RTX A5000 with 24GB GPU memory, CUDA v11.3 and cuDNN v8.3.2 in PyTorch v1.12.1.

elements.sent-2411-258: For the convex problem, we choose the Adult dataset from the UCI repository \citep{misc_adult_2 , which records adult incomes given 99 attributes.}
elements.sent-2411-259: The input features are normalized using $l_1$ norm.
elements.sent-2411-260: A linear regression model is optimized by minimizing the mean squared error (MSE).
elements.sent-2411-261: We adopt SGD with batch size 60.
elements.sent-2411-262: For the constant learning rate case, the learning rate is set to $0.2$.
elements.sent-2411-263: For the decaying learning rate case, the learning rate starts from $0.4$, and decays four times uniformaly during the training, e.g., $\alpha_t = \frac{0.4 {\sqrt{t , t=1, 2, 3, 4$, so the the learning rate is converged to $0.2$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-264: For both cases, the model is optimized for 100 epochs.
elements.sent-2411-265: For the FWA method, the averaging step $k$ is chosen from $\{1, 100, 1000, 5000, T\}$.
elements.sent-2411-266: Specifically, for $k = 1$, FWA reduces to SGD, while for $k = T$, all the historical models are averaged.
elements.sent-2411-267: Then we consider the non-convex problem of training LeNet \cite{lecun1998gradient on the CIFAR10 dataset \cite{krizhevsky2009learning .}}
elements.sent-2411-268: Similarly, we adopt SGD with batch size 60.
elements.sent-2411-269: For the constant learning rate case, the learning rate is set to $0.1$.
elements.sent-2411-270: For the decaying learning rate case, the learning rate starts from $0.2$, and then decays to $0.1$ as in the convex setting.
elements.sent-2411-271: The model is also optimized for 100 epochs.
elements.sent-2411-272: Additionally, the averaging step $k$ is chosen from $\{1, 10, 30, 50\}$.
elements.sent-2411-273: For the stability analysis, we follow the setting of \cite{hardt2016train .}
elements.sent-2411-274: In specific, the stability is measured by the parameter distances trained on two different datasets and the generalization error.
elements.sent-2411-275: Smaller parameter differences and generalization error indicate stronger stability.
elements.sent-2411-276: To construct two different datasets, we randomly remove one sample from the training set to construct dataset $S$.
elements.sent-2411-277: Then, another dataset $S'$ is constructed by replacing one random data point in $S$ with the deleted one.
elements.sent-2411-278: We train the model on these two datasets with the same initialization and settings.
elements.sent-2411-279: The parameter distance is defined as the Euclidean distance between parameters of the two models, e.g., $\sqrt{\Vert w-w^{\prime}\Vert^2/(\Vert w \Vert^2 + \Vert w^{\prime}\Vert^2)}$, where $w$ and $w^{\prime}$ denote all the parameters of models trained on $S$ and $S'$ respectively.
